judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit every expectation with well-cited commit history; they are comparable, with Report 2 slightly more thorough on packaging/driver gaps.

report_1.evidence: this migration was performed once under PLAT-31 (`git show 454a21f`, `597777b`)

report_2.evidence: `git log --oneline` in `carelog-api` shows commits `454a21f` "Add Edgerunner serverless deploy for API (PLAT-31)", `597777b` "Open a dedicated Postgres connection per request in the serverless handler (PLAT-31)", and `1712565` "Revert Edgerunner migration…

report_1.evidence: reverted (`1712565`) after taking `/reports/monthly` down in production. The specific risk ... Under per-request connections, "normal dashboard load" opened connections faster than Postgres recycled them → `FATAL: too many connections` → endpoint down (`1712565`)

report_2.evidence: Commit `1712565`: "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; `/reports/monthly` exhausted Postgres `max_connections` in production." The body adds that "per-request connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them and tripped the connection limit (`FATAL: too many connections`), taking the endpoint down."

report_1.evidence: attempted, run in production, caused an outage (`FATAL: too many connections`), and deliberately reverted back to Cargobox one week later

report_2.evidence: this migration was executed end-to-end, caused a production outage (`FATAL: too many connections`, endpoint down), and was reverted under PLAT-31

report_1.evidence: It should go to the owner of PLAT-31 / the platform decision, who reverted it as a production call — the prior attempt and revert are information they need.

report_2.evidence: the platform/infra owner of PLAT-31 (author of the reverted attempt ...) ... Because this touches shared database capacity and has a prior production outage, it is explicitly not a solo decision.
